_meta.description: PetCare illness knowledge base for RAG-grounded triage. Each entry provides symptom cues, urgency signals, species context, and differential indicators to ground LLM triage decisions.
sources: ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center guidelines
AVMA (American Veterinary Medical Association) triage protocols
Cornell Feline Health Center symptom guidance
Veterinary Emergency and Critical Care Society (VECCS) urgency criteria
_meta.usage: Retrieved by keyword overlap at triage time. Top-k matches injected into triage LLM system prompt as reference context.

illnesses.name: Acute Gastroenteritis
urgency_escalators: vomiting more than 3 times in 24 hours
not eating for more than 24 hours
illnesses.key_triage_notes: Single vomiting episode in an otherwise normal adult dog or cat with no blood is usually Soon/Routine. Multiple episodes, bloody discharge, or concurrent diarrhea + lethargy → Same-day. Any suspected foreign body or toxin → Emergency.

species_notes.cat: Cats hide GI discomfort well; by the time vomiting is noticed, condition may be more advanced. Even one episode in a lethargic cat warrants Same-day evaluation.
species_notes.dog: Dietary indiscretion (garbage, foreign food) is common. Monitor for bloat (GDV) in large deep-chested breeds — bloated firm abdomen after eating is Emergency.

illnesses.name: Gastric Dilatation-Volvulus (GDV / Bloat)
illnesses.key_triage_notes: GDV is always Emergency. Stomach twists on itself cutting off blood supply — fatal without surgery within hours. Large deep-chested breeds (Great Dane, German Shepherd, Standard Poodle, Weimaraner, Boxer) at highest risk. Hard distended abdomen + retching with nothing coming up = go to emergency vet NOW.

species_notes.dog: High-risk breeds: Great Dane, German Shepherd, Standard Poodle, Weimaraner, Boxer, Rottweiler, Irish Wolfhound. Never wait-and-see with GDV symptoms.

illnesses.name: Foreign Body Ingestion / Obstruction
illnesses.key_triage_notes: If owner witnesses ingestion of string, bone, sharp object, or large non-food item → Same-day minimum, possibly Emergency if symptoms present. Cats who swallow string/thread/ribbon are at high risk for intestinal perforation — Emergency.

species_notes.cat: Linear foreign bodies (string, thread, ribbon, hair tie) wrap around the base of the tongue or anchor in the stomach while intestines bunch — requires emergency surgery.
species_notes.dog: Corn cobs are extremely dangerous — they do not break down and cause complete obstruction. Cooked bones can splinter.

illnesses.name: Respiratory Distress
illnesses.key_triage_notes: Any change in gum color (blue, grey, pale) + breathing difficulty = Emergency. Open mouth breathing in a cat is always Emergency — cats should never pant like dogs. Mild cough alone in an otherwise normal pet can be Soon.

species_notes.cat: Cats should NEVER pant or breathe with open mouths. Any open-mouth breathing in a cat is a respiratory emergency. Cats with pleural effusion (fluid around lungs) often breathe with their chest only, avoiding belly movement.
species_notes.dog: Brachycephalic breeds (Bulldog, Pug, Shih Tzu, French Bulldog) have baseline respiratory noise but acute worsening is serious. Laryngeal paralysis in older large breeds causes progressive breathing difficulty.

illnesses.name: Kennel Cough (Infectious Tracheobronchitis)
urgency_de_escalators: just returned from boarding or dog park
illnesses.key_triage_notes: Classic goose-honk cough post-boarding in a healthy vaccinated adult dog is typically Routine. Add lethargy, fever, or yellow discharge → Soon or Same-day (possible pneumonia). Puppies and immunocompromised dogs warrant more urgent evaluation.

species_notes.dog: Highly contagious. Isolate from other dogs. Vaccination (Bordetella) reduces severity. Most adult healthy dogs recover in 1–3 weeks.

illnesses.name: Seizures / Epilepsy
urgency_escalators: cluster seizures (more than one in 24 hours)
post-ictal confusion lasting more than 30 minutes
urgency_de_escalators: brief seizure (under 90 seconds) that resolved fully
illnesses.key_triage_notes: First-ever seizure is always Emergency regardless of how the pet looks now — underlying cause must be identified. Cluster seizures or continuous seizure (status epilepticus) is life-threatening. Known epileptics with a single typical brief seizure and full recovery can be evaluated urgently (same-day) rather than emergency if stable.

species_notes.dog: Most common cause is idiopathic epilepsy in otherwise healthy young-to-middle-aged dogs. Always rule out toxins, hypoglycemia, and structural brain disease.
species_notes.cat: Seizures in cats are less often idiopathic — metabolic causes (liver disease, hypertension, hyperthyroidism), FIP, and toxins are common. Always warrant same-day or emergency evaluation.

illnesses.name: Urinary Blockage (FLUTD / Urethral Obstruction)
urgency_escalators: male cat straining with no output
no urine produced in more than 12 hours
crying or vocalizing when trying to urinate
urgency_de_escalators: female cat with some urine output
small amount of blood without straining
illnesses.key_triage_notes: Male cats with urethral obstruction can die within 24–48 hours from kidney failure and cardiac toxicity. ANY male cat straining with little or no urine output = Emergency, no exceptions. Female cats are anatomically less prone to blockage but can still have FLUTD. Blood-only without straining can be Soon/Same-day in otherwise normal female cats.
red_flags: male cat straining with no output

species_notes.cat: Male cats have a narrow urethra — mineral crystals or mucus plugs cause life-threatening obstruction. Emergency signs: straining, crying, distended bladder you can feel, lethargy, vomiting.
species_notes.dog: Urinary blockage in dogs is less common but males can obstruct with stones. Straining with no output in any male dog = Emergency.

illnesses.name: Urinary Tract Infection (UTI)
urgency_escalators: male cat with no urine output
illnesses.key_triage_notes: Uncomplicated lower UTI in a dog producing urine and otherwise normal = Soon. Add fever, back pain, or vomiting → Same-day (possible kidney involvement). Always rule out blockage in male cats first.

species_notes.dog: Female dogs more prone to UTI. Recurrent UTIs may indicate stones, anatomical abnormality, or diabetes.
species_notes.cat: Cats often have stress-related FLUTD (feline idiopathic cystitis) without infection. Environmental modification plus pain management often more important than antibiotics.

illnesses.name: Skin Infection / Hot Spot (Acute Moist Dermatitis)
urgency_escalators: not eating due to skin pain
illnesses.key_triage_notes: Small localized hot spots or mild itching are Routine. Rapidly spreading, deeply infected, or causing systemic signs (fever, lymph node swelling) warrant Same-day. Any open wound with signs of deep infection needs same-day or urgent evaluation.

species_notes.dog: Hot spots are very common in summer, especially in dense-coated breeds (Golden Retriever, German Shepherd). Usually secondary to allergies, flea bites, or moisture trapped under fur.
species_notes.cat: Cats over-groom from stress or pain — symmetric hair loss without obvious lesions often indicates psychogenic alopecia or pain from another cause.

illnesses.name: Allergic Reaction / Anaphylaxis
urgency_escalators: known bee sting or snake bite
illnesses.key_triage_notes: Facial/muzzle swelling after a sting or bite = Same-day minimum. Any breathing difficulty with swelling = Emergency (anaphylaxis). Hives-only without swelling in otherwise normal pet can be Soon if monitored closely.

species_notes.dog: Dogs can have anaphylaxis from bee stings, vaccines, or medications. Vomiting within minutes of a sting + collapse = Emergency.
species_notes.cat: Cats less commonly have dramatic anaphylaxis but can develop facial swelling and respiratory distress.

illnesses.name: Limping / Lameness
illnesses.key_triage_notes: Mild limp with some weight-bearing and no trauma = Soon. Non-weight-bearing, trauma, suspected fracture = Same-day or Emergency. Cat with sudden hind limb paralysis (aortic thromboembolism / saddle thrombus) = Emergency — cold, painful, paralyzed hind limbs.

species_notes.dog: Common causes: soft tissue sprain, cruciate ligament tear (often gradual), hip dysplasia (older large breeds). Acute severe lameness post-exercise in young dogs: rule out panosteitis or osteosarcoma in predisposed breeds.
species_notes.cat: Sudden bilateral hind limb weakness/paralysis with cold painful limbs = aortic saddle thrombus — Emergency, often secondary to hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.

illnesses.name: Cardiovascular / Heart Disease
urgency_escalators: very rapid or very slow heart rate
illnesses.key_triage_notes: New onset of exercise intolerance + coughing in an older dog warrants Same-day. Collapse, fainting, breathing difficulty at rest = Emergency. Known heart disease patient with mild new coughing should be seen same-day — may signal worsening.
red_flags: breathing difficulty at rest with cardiac history

species_notes.dog: Cavalier King Charles Spaniels, Dachshunds, Boxers predisposed to cardiac disease. Mitral valve disease most common in small breed dogs.
species_notes.cat: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) is common in cats, often hidden. Maine Coons and Ragdolls genetically predisposed. First sign may be hind limb paralysis (saddle thrombus) or acute respiratory distress.

illnesses.name: Toxin Ingestion — General
illnesses.key_triage_notes: Any known or suspected toxin ingestion = Emergency. Do NOT wait for symptoms. Time to decontamination (induce vomiting, activated charcoal) is critical. Always ask: what substance, how much, how long ago. Contact ASPCA Poison Control (888-426-4435) or Pet Poison Helpline in parallel.

species_notes.dog: Common toxins: chocolate, xylitol (sugar-free gum/candy), grapes/raisins, onions/garlic, macadamia nuts, ibuprofen/acetaminophen, rat poison (rodenticide), antifreeze (ethylene glycol).
species_notes.cat: Cats are extremely sensitive to: acetaminophen (Tylenol) — even one pill can be fatal, lilies (all parts cause acute kidney failure), permethrin (flea products for dogs), essential oils, aspirin.

illnesses.name: Chocolate Toxicity
urgency_escalators: large amount relative to body weight
illnesses.key_triage_notes: Dark and baking chocolate contain high theobromine — toxic even in small amounts in small dogs. Always Emergency regardless of whether the pet 'seems fine' — symptoms can take 6–12 hours to appear. White chocolate has very low toxicity. Milk chocolate is intermediate.
red_flags: dark or baking chocolate ingestion in any amount

species_notes.dog: Theobromine cannot be metabolized efficiently by dogs. Clinical signs: vomiting, diarrhea, restlessness, excessive urination, muscle tremors, seizures, cardiac arrhythmias.

illnesses.name: Lily Toxicity (Cats)
urgency_escalators: any lily exposure in a cat
illnesses.key_triage_notes: ALL parts of true lilies (Lilium and Hemerocallis species) cause acute kidney failure in cats — pollen, leaves, petals, water from the vase. Even small exposure = Emergency. Onset of kidney failure can occur within 24–72 hours. If cat has eaten any part of a lily plant, induce vomiting immediately and go to ER.

species_notes.cat: Peace lily and calla lily cause oral irritation but not kidney failure. True lilies (Easter, Tiger, Asiatic, Daylily) = Emergency. When in doubt, treat all lily ingestion in cats as Emergency.

illnesses.name: Xylitol Toxicity
urgency_escalators: any xylitol ingestion in a dog
illnesses.key_triage_notes: Xylitol causes rapid insulin release → severe hypoglycemia and potentially liver failure in dogs. Found in sugar-free gum, candy, some peanut butters, baked goods, mouthwash. Even 1–2 pieces of gum can cause hypoglycemia in a small dog. Always Emergency.

species_notes.dog: Signs of xylitol toxicity: vomiting, loss of coordination, lethargy, seizures within 30–60 minutes. Liver failure can develop 8–12 hours later.

illnesses.name: Diabetes — Hypoglycemia
illnesses.key_triage_notes: Known diabetic pet with neurological signs after insulin administration = Emergency hypoglycemia. Rub corn syrup or honey on gums en route to ER. Do not give food — may aspirate. Any diabetic pet that is unresponsive or seizing = Emergency.

species_notes.dog: Usually overdose of insulin. If pet received insulin but did not eat, risk of hypoglycemia is high.
species_notes.cat: Cats with remission can have ongoing hypoglycemia if insulin dose not adjusted. Wobbliness, dilated pupils, lethargy = hypoglycemia.

illnesses.name: Hypothyroidism (Dog) / Hyperthyroidism (Cat)
urgency_escalators: heart murmur or rapid heart rate (cat hyperthyroid)
illnesses.key_triage_notes: Gradual weight loss + increased appetite in middle-aged to older cat = hyperthyroidism (very common). Routine workup unless cardiac symptoms present (rapid heart rate, murmur). Hypothyroidism in dogs causes weight gain and lethargy — Routine workup.
red_flags: sudden blindness in cat with suspected hyperthyroidism

species_notes.dog: Hypothyroidism common in middle-aged medium-large breeds (Golden Retriever, Labrador, Doberman). Causes weight gain, lethargy, bilateral symmetric hair loss.
species_notes.cat: Hyperthyroidism most common endocrine disease in cats over 10 years. Can cause hypertension leading to retinal detachment and sudden blindness — Same-day if vision changes noted.

illnesses.name: Eye Injury / Proptosis / Corneal Ulcer
illnesses.key_triage_notes: Eye prolapse (globe out of socket) — most common in brachycephalic dogs (Pug, Shih Tzu, Bulldog) after trauma = Emergency. Severe squinting or cloudiness after trauma = Same-day. Mild discharge without squinting = Routine.

species_notes.dog: Brachycephalic breeds at high risk for globe prolapse. Any trauma to the face in these breeds warrants immediate evaluation.
species_notes.cat: Herpesvirus causes recurrent eye issues. Upper respiratory signs with eye discharge is common. Corneal ulcers are painful — seen as severe squinting.

illnesses.name: Parvovirus (Canine)
illnesses.key_triage_notes: Unvaccinated puppy with vomiting + bloody diarrhea = parvo until proven otherwise = Emergency. Parvo kills rapidly through dehydration and secondary infection. Highly contagious — isolate from other dogs. Even vaccinated puppies can contract parvo if series is incomplete.
red_flags: vomiting + diarrhea in puppy under 6 months

species_notes.dog: Most common in puppies 6 weeks – 6 months. Highest risk: unvaccinated, shelter/rescue, contact with infected dogs. Mortality without treatment: up to 90%. With aggressive treatment: 70–80% survival.

illnesses.name: Pancreatitis
illnesses.key_triage_notes: Vomiting + abdominal pain + recent high-fat meal = pancreatitis until proven otherwise → Same-day. Severe pancreatitis can cause shock and multi-organ failure → Emergency. Miniature Schnauzers and Cocker Spaniels are predisposed.

species_notes.dog: Often triggered by fatty foods (holiday meals, table scraps). Miniature Schnauzers have high genetic predisposition.
species_notes.cat: Triaditis (pancreatitis + IBD + cholangiohepatitis) common in cats. Often more subtle than dogs.

illnesses.name: Anal Gland Issues
urgency_escalators: abscess (swollen hot painful area near anus)
illnesses.key_triage_notes: Scooting and licking rear = anal gland discomfort, typically Routine for expression. Swollen, hot, painful area near anus = possible abscess = Same-day. Rupture of anal gland abscess (open wound near anus) = Same-day treatment needed.

species_notes.dog: Very common, especially in small breeds. Regular expression (every 3–8 weeks) prevents impaction. Small breeds, overweight dogs predisposed.
species_notes.cat: Less common but can occur. Cats rarely scoot — more likely to over-groom tail base.

illnesses.name: Dental Disease / Tooth Abscess
urgency_escalators: facial swelling under eye (tooth root abscess)
not eating due to mouth pain
illnesses.key_triage_notes: Bad breath and tartar alone = Routine dental cleaning. Facial swelling under the eye in dogs = tooth root abscess = Soon or Same-day (needs antibiotics and dental treatment). Refusing to eat due to mouth pain = Soon.

species_notes.dog: Carnassial tooth (upper 4th premolar) root abscess presents as swelling below the eye. Common in medium-large dogs.
species_notes.cat: Tooth resorption is very common in cats — painful but may not be visible. Cats hiding, not eating, chattering jaw = dental pain.

illnesses.name: Pyometra
urgency_de_escalators: open-cervix with discharge (less immediately life-threatening but still urgent)
illnesses.key_triage_notes: Pyometra (uterine infection) in intact female dog or cat 4–8 weeks after heat cycle = Emergency. Closed pyometra (no discharge, uterus sealed) is life-threatening — toxin accumulation and uterine rupture possible. Open pyometra with vaginal discharge = Same-day at minimum, Emergency if systemic signs present.
red_flags: intact female with lethargy + not eating + recent heat cycle

species_notes.dog: Most common in dogs over 6 years old, typically 1–8 weeks after heat. Signs: lethargy, not eating, excessive drinking, vaginal discharge (or absent in closed form).
species_notes.cat: Less common than in dogs but occurs. Same urgency classification applies.

illnesses.name: Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD)
urgency_escalators: loss of bladder or bowel control
illnesses.key_triage_notes: Back pain with intact walking = Soon or Same-day. Any loss of function in hind limbs (weakness, dragging, paralysis) or loss of bladder/bowel control = Emergency — spinal cord compression must be decompressed within hours for best recovery. Dachshunds and Corgis are highly predisposed.
red_flags: loss of bladder or bowel control
sudden onset back pain with neurological signs

species_notes.dog: Chondrodystrophic breeds (Dachshund, Basset Hound, Corgi, Pekingese, Beagle) extremely predisposed to IVDD. Even minor back trauma can cause disc herniation.
species_notes.cat: Less common than dogs but can occur. Aortic thromboembolism (saddle thrombus) can mimic IVDD — check limb warmth and femoral pulse.
